topic: Unit 3: Extended Writing - Film and Literature
subject: french

items.q: When does the past participle agree with avoir verbs?
items.correct: When the direct object comes BEFORE the verb (la pomme que j'ai mangée).
items.why: Students learn 'avoir = no agreement' as a blanket rule. The exception is PRECEDING DIRECT OBJECT: 'Je l'ai mangée' (la pomme) requires the participle to agree with the object pronoun.

items.q: How do you say 'I am going to Spain' in French?
items.why: Students confuse aller (to go - movement away from speaker) and venir (to come - movement towards speaker). 'Je vais' = I'm going; 'je viens' = I'm coming. Also: en + feminine country, au + masculine, aux + plural.

Students default to à. The rule: EN for feminine countries (most ending -e: France, Italie, Espagne); AU for masculine (au Portugal, au Canada); AUX for plural (aux États-Unis). À is for cities (à Paris).
It's Meursault. “Rieux” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which form is the correct imparfait of 'être' for 'il'?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: était. “est” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year did Camus win the Nobel Prize for Literature?
items.why: Exact answer: 1957. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
